questions.question: The Atal Pension Yojana (APY) targets:
options: Unorganized sector workers — provides guaranteed pension of Rs 1,000-5,000/month at age 60; administered by PFRDA; co-contribution by government for eligible subscribers
questions.explanation: APY (2015, replaced Swavalamban scheme): for unorganized sector workers. Subscribers aged 18-40 contribute monthly; at 60, receive guaranteed pension of Rs 1,000/2,000/3,000/4,000/5,000 per month. Government (co-contribution of 50% of contribution or Rs 1,000/year, whichever lower) for eligible subscribers for 5 years. Administered by PFRDA (Pension Fund Regulatory and Development Authority).

questions.question: The UDAN (Ude Desh Ka Aam Nagrik) scheme is related to:
options: Regional Air Connectivity Scheme — makes air travel affordable on unserved/underserved routes; caps airfare for 1-hour flight; launched 2016
questions.explanation: UDAN (Ude Desh Ka Aam Nagrik — Let the Common Citizen Fly) 2016: provides affordable air connectivity to Tier-2/Tier-3 cities. Viability Gap Funding (VGF) provided to airlines operating on unserved routes. Capped airfare: Rs 2,500 for 1-hour flight. Revived and developed many regional airports. Over 400 routes operationalized.

questions.question: The Ayushman Bharat PM-JAY scheme provides:
options: Health insurance of Rs 5 lakh per family per year for secondary and tertiary hospitalization to ~10 crore poor/vulnerable families (50 crore beneficiaries) — launched 2018
questions.explanation: Ayushman Bharat - Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (PM-JAY, 2018): world's largest health assurance scheme. Covers Rs 5 lakh per family per year for hospitalization. Target: 10.74 crore poor/vulnerable families (50 crore beneficiaries) — bottom 40% of population. Cashless treatment at empanelled private and public hospitals. SECC 2011 data used for beneficiary identification.

questions.question: The Sarva Shiksha Abhiyan (SSA) was launched to:
options: Universalize elementary education (Classes 1-8) — free and compulsory education for all children 6-14 years; merged into Samagra Shiksha in 2018
questions.explanation: SSA (2001): flagship program for universalizing elementary education (RTE Act 2009 gave it constitutional backing). Established/upgraded schools in unserved habitations, recruited teachers, built classrooms. Mid-Day Meal Scheme complemented SSA. In 2018, SSA, RMSA (secondary), and Teacher Education merged into Samagra Shiksha Abhiyan.

questions.question: The National Pension System (NPS) replaced the Old Pension Scheme (OPS) for Central Government employees from:
options: January 1, 2004 (for employees joining on/after that date) — contributory scheme where employee contributes 10% and government 14% of basic+DA; managed by PFRDA
questions.explanation: NPS (National Pension System, 2004): replaced defined benefit OPS for central government employees joining from January 1, 2004 onwards. Contributory (employee: 10%, employer: 14% for central govt). Market-linked returns. Managed by PFRDA. NPS extended to all citizens in 2009 (Swavalamban/NPS-Lite for unorganized sector).

questions.question: Pradhan Mantri Suraksha Bima Yojana (PMSBY) provides:
options: Accidental death/disability insurance cover of Rs 2 lakh at Rs 20 premium per year — for bank account holders aged 18-70; launched 2015
questions.explanation: PMSBY (2015): accidental death/permanent total disability: Rs 2 lakh; permanent partial disability: Rs 1 lakh. Annual premium: just Rs 20 (for 18-70 year olds with bank accounts). Auto-debit from bank account. Covers road accidents, drowning, accidental falls etc. Part of Jan Suraksha schemes. Pradhan Mantri Jeevan Jyoti Bima Yojana (PMJJBY): life insurance Rs 2 lakh, Rs 436/year.

questions.question: The 'poverty trap' is a situation where:
options: Poor people cannot escape poverty because they lack resources to invest in health, education, or productive assets — low income → low savings → low investment → low income (vicious cycle)
Poverty is self-fulfilling prophecy due to geography
questions.explanation: Poverty Trap (Jeffrey Sachs): poor people/countries stuck in poverty because: too poor to save/invest → capital doesn't accumulate → productivity stays low → income stays low. Solutions: 'Big Push' (massive external aid to break the cycle), conditional cash transfers, microfinance. Counter-view (Daron Acemoglu, James Robinson): institutions matter more than external shocks.

questions.question: The concept of 'disguised unemployment' is different from 'seasonal unemployment' in that:
options: Disguised unemployment: excess labor with zero marginal product (mainly in agriculture, year-round). Seasonal unemployment: workers unemployed during off-season (only part of year)
questions.explanation: Disguised Unemployment (structural): workers appear employed but contribute zero output (farm has 5 workers when 3 suffice). Zero marginal product. Removing 2 workers doesn't reduce output. Seasonal Unemployment: workers unemployed part of year (farmers during off-season, tourism workers in lean season). Cyclic unemployment: due to recession. Frictional: between jobs.

questions.question: The Integrated Child Development Services (ICDS) scheme provides:
options: Package of 6 services to children 0-6 years and pregnant/lactating mothers: supplementary nutrition, immunization, health check-up, referral services, pre-school education, nutrition & health education
questions.explanation: ICDS (1975): world's largest and most comprehensive early childhood development program. 6 services through Anganwadi Centres: (1) Supplementary nutrition, (2) Immunization, (3) Health check-up, (4) Referral services, (5) Pre-school non-formal education (3-6 years), (6) Nutrition & health education (for women). 1.4 million Anganwadis, 14 crore beneficiaries.

questions.question: The index 'Multidimensional Poverty Index (MPI)' measures poverty using:
options: Three dimensions: health (nutrition, child mortality), education (years of schooling, school attendance), and living standards (cooking fuel, sanitation, water, electricity, housing, assets) — UNDP/OPHI
questions.explanation: MPI (UNDP and OPHI — Oxford Poverty and Human Development Initiative): multidimensional poverty measure using 10 indicators across 3 dimensions. A household is 'deprived' in each indicator; 'multidimensionally poor' if deprived in >33% of weighted indicators. India's MPI: 415 million exited poverty 2005-06 to 2019-21 (massive reduction). India publishes National MPI annually.

questions.question: India's 'One Nation One Ration Card' (ONORC) scheme allows:
options: PDS (ration card) beneficiaries to claim their subsidized food entitlement from any Fair Price Shop in India — especially beneficial for migrant workers who move to different states for work
questions.explanation: ONORC (2019-2021, nationwide rollout): using Aadhaar-linked biometric authentication at FPS (Fair Price Shop), beneficiary can claim PDS quota from any FPS in India. Critical for: migrant workers (can claim entitlement at destination state), seasonal migrants. All 36 states/UTs joined ONORC by 2021. 80+ crore beneficiaries. Managed through Integrated Management of PDS (IM-PDS) portal.

questions.question: The Startup India Seed Fund Scheme (SISFS) provides:
options: Rs 945 crore seed funding to early-stage startups through incubators — for proof of concept, prototype development, trials, market entry; Max Rs 20 lakh grant or Rs 50 lakh soft loan per startup
questions.explanation: SISFS (2021-25): Rs 945 crore over 4 years. Grants through selected incubators: Rs 20 lakh for proof of concept/validation (PoC, prototype, trials); Rs 50 lakh as soft loan for commercialization/market entry. Incubators apply to DPIIT; approved incubators select startups. Fills the gap between angel investment and formal VC: 'seed funding' stage.

questions.question: Frictional unemployment occurs when:
options: Workers are between jobs or searching for better jobs
Jobs exist but skills don't match
questions.explanation: Frictional unemployment: temporary unemployment while workers transition between jobs or search for better ones. It's natural and exists even in a healthy economy (part of 'natural rate of unemployment').

questions.question: The Soil Health Card scheme launched in 2015 aims to:
options: Provide farmers with soil nutrient status and fertilizer recommendations
questions.explanation: Soil Health Card: issued every 2 years to farmers; provides information on 12 parameters (N, P, K, pH, micronutrients) with crop-wise recommendations to optimize fertilizer use.

questions.question: Kisan Credit Card (KCC) scheme provides short-term credit to farmers for:
options: Crop cultivation, post-harvest expenses, maintenance, and allied activities
questions.explanation: KCC (1998, NABARD): provides revolving credit for crop cultivation, post-harvest expenses, allied activities (fisheries, animal husbandry since 2018-19), and maintenance needs at subsidized rates.

questions.question: Which index measures the inequality of income or wealth distribution in a country?
questions.explanation: Gini Coefficient (Corrado Gini, 1912): 0 = perfect equality; 1 = maximum inequality. Derived from Lorenz Curve. India's Gini ~0.36 (income). Used alongside HDI to measure inclusive development.

questions.question: The Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY) is a scheme for:
options: Crop insurance — provides financial support to farmers suffering crop loss due to natural calamities, pests, or disease; launched 2016, low premium (2% for kharif, 1.5% rabi)
questions.explanation: PMFBY (February 2016): replaced NAIS and MNAIS. Features: uniform premium of 2% for kharif crops, 1.5% for rabi crops, 5% for commercial/horticultural crops; remaining premium paid by Centre and State equally. Uses satellite, remote sensing, and smartphone apps for rapid loss assessment. Covers pre-sowing to post-harvest losses.

questions.question: India's Urban Employment Guarantee Scheme (UGEGS) for urban poor was introduced in:
options: 2020 during COVID-19 pandemic as 'PM Garib Kalyan Rojgar Abhiyan' for migrant workers — some states like Rajasthan have their own urban employment guarantee schemes
questions.explanation: Urban employment guarantee: the PM Garib Kalyan Rojgar Abhiyan (June 2020) provided 125 days of work to returning migrant workers in 116 districts. Rajasthan's Indira Gandhi Shahri Rozgar Guarantee Yojana (2022): 100 days urban employment. Kerala has similar scheme. No national-level urban employment guarantee act yet.

questions.question: POSHAN 2.0 (2021) merged which earlier schemes?
options: POSHAN Abhiyaan and Supplementary Nutrition Programme and other nutrition schemes
questions.explanation: POSHAN 2.0: merged POSHAN Abhiyaan, ICDS (Supplementary Nutrition), National Creche Scheme, Kishori Shakti Yojana into an integrated nutrition support programme under Mission Saksham Anganwadi.

questions.question: PM POSHAN scheme (renamed from Mid-Day Meal in 2021) covers students up to:
questions.explanation: PM POSHAN: covers students from pre-primary (Balvatika) to Class VIII in government and government-aided schools. ~11.8 crore students across ~11.2 lakh schools.

questions.question: Ayushman Bharat PM-JAY (Jan Arogya Yojana) provides health cover of how much per family per year?
questions.explanation: PM-JAY (launched September 23, 2018): Rs 5 lakh per family per year for secondary and tertiary care hospitalization. World's largest government health insurance scheme. ~55 crore beneficiaries (50 crore poor).

questions.question: The Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY) launched in 2016 replaced which earlier crop insurance schemes?
options: NAIS (National Agricultural Insurance Scheme) and MNAIS
questions.explanation: PMFBY (January 2016): replaced NAIS (1999) and MNAIS (2010). Premium rates: Kharif 2%, Rabi 1.5%, commercial/horticultural crops 5%. Actuarial premium difference borne by Centre and State.

questions.question: Which committee recommended the rationalization of centrally sponsored schemes (CSS) in India?
questions.explanation: B.K. Chaturvedi Committee (2011): recommended rationalizing and restructuring CSS. Later, Sub-Group of CMs under CM Shivraj Singh Chouhan (2015) recommended reducing CSS from 66 to 28 for more flexibility.

questions.question: What is the primary objective of monetary policy in the context of poverty and inclusion in India?
options: A) Price stability and economic growth
questions.explanation: Monetary policy aims to achieve price stability and promote economic growth, which can help reduce poverty.

questions.question: Which of the following fiscal indicators is most closely related to poverty reduction in India?
options: A) Fiscal deficit as a percentage of GDP
C) Capital expenditure on social sectors
questions.explanation: Capital expenditure on social sectors directly impacts poverty reduction.

questions.question: How does rupee depreciation affect the poor in India?
options: C) Has no impact on their purchasing power
D) Leads to higher employment opportunities
questions.explanation: Rupee depreciation increases import prices, reducing the purchasing power of the poor.

questions.question: What is the impact of sterilized interventions by the RBI on poverty?
options: A) Reduces poverty by increasing money supply
B) Increases poverty by reducing money supply
C) Has no direct impact on poverty
D) Leads to higher economic growth
questions.explanation: Sterilized interventions have no direct impact on poverty.

questions.question: Which component of the balance of payments is most relevant to poverty reduction in India?

questions.question: How does inflation affect the poor in India?
options: C) Has no impact on their real income

questions.question: How does a trade deficit affect the poor in India?
options: C) Has no impact on their employment opportunities
D) Leads to higher economic growth
questions.explanation: Trade deficit can lead to reduced employment opportunities.

questions.question: What is the impact of fiscal policy on poverty reduction in India?
options: C) Can reduce poverty if targeted correctly
D) Has no impact on poverty
questions.explanation: Fiscal policy can reduce poverty if targeted correctly.

questions.question: What is the primary objective of monetary policy in the context of poverty reduction in India?
questions.explanation: Monetary policy aims to control inflation, which disproportionately affects the poor. Low inflation supports poverty reduction.

questions.question: Which of the following fiscal indicators is most relevant to assessing poverty inclusion in India?
questions.explanation: Social sector expenditure directly impacts poverty inclusion by allocating funds to welfare schemes.

questions.question: Agricultural reforms can contribute to poverty reduction by
options: B) Enhancing market access for farmers
questions.explanation: Better market access for farmers can increase their incomes, thus reducing poverty.

questions.question: Inflation can exacerbate poverty in India because it
options: A) Increases the purchasing power of the poor
B) Reduces the cost of living
C) Decreases the value of savings
D) Erodes the real income of the poor
questions.explanation: Inflation reduces the purchasing power of the poor, making them poorer.

questions.question: A trade deficit can impact poverty in India by
options: C) Reducing imports of essential goods
D) Decreasing the value of the rupee
questions.explanation: A depreciating rupee increases import prices, affecting the poor who spend a larger portion of their income on essentials.

questions.question: Sterilized interventions by the RBI are used to
questions.explanation: Sterilized interventions absorb excess liquidity to prevent inflation.

questions.question: The balance of payments (BOP) is closely related to poverty because it affects
options: A) Foreign investment in poverty alleviation programs
B) The value of the rupee and thus import prices
C) The government's ability to fund social welfare schemes
D) The level of foreign aid received
questions.explanation: BOP affects the rupee's value, which in turn impacts import prices and the cost of living for the poor.

questions.question: Rupee depreciation can affect poverty by
options: A) Increasing the cost of imports
B) Reducing the cost of exports
questions.explanation: Rupee depreciation increases import costs, affecting the poor who rely on imported goods.

questions.question: Social indicators such as the Human Development Index (HDI) are important for assessing poverty inclusion because they
options: B) Reflect the standard of living
C) Capture multidimensional aspects of well-being
D) Focus solely on income poverty
questions.explanation: HDI captures aspects of well-being beyond income, providing a comprehensive view of poverty.

questions.question: Fiscal policy can directly address poverty through
options: B) Public expenditure on social sectors
questions.explanation: Public expenditure on social sectors directly benefits the poor and vulnerable populations.

questions.question: What is the expected impact of rupee depreciation on poverty and inclusion in India?
options: Increase in exports and reduction in poverty
Decrease in imports and increase in poverty
Increase in inflation and reduction in poverty
Decrease in economic growth and increase in poverty
questions.explanation: Rupee depreciation can lead to increase in exports, which can create jobs and reduce poverty.

questions.question: Which of the following agricultural reforms is most likely to benefit small and marginal farmers in India?
questions.explanation: Agricultural marketing reforms can help small farmers sell their produce at better prices, reducing poverty.

questions.question: What is the relationship between inflation and poverty in India?
options: Inflation has no impact on poverty
Inflation is not related to poverty
questions.explanation: Inflation can reduce the purchasing power of the poor, increasing poverty.

questions.question: Which of the following trade balances is most closely related to poverty and inclusion in India?
questions.explanation: Trade deficit can lead to reduction in economic growth, increasing poverty.

questions.question: What is the impact of sterilized interventions by the RBI on poverty and inclusion in India?
options: Increase in economic growth and reduction in poverty
No impact on poverty and inclusion
questions.explanation: Sterilized interventions can help reduce inflation, which can reduce poverty.

questions.question: Which of the following social indicators is most closely related to poverty and inclusion in India?
questions.explanation: Poverty headcount ratio is a direct measure of poverty, making it closely related to poverty and inclusion.

questions.question: What is the expected impact of increase in foreign direct investment on poverty and inclusion in India?
options: Increase in economic growth and reduction in poverty
Decrease in economic growth and increase in poverty
No impact on poverty and inclusion
questions.explanation: Increase in FDI can lead to increase in economic growth, creating jobs and reducing poverty.

questions.question: Which of the following balance of payments components is most closely related to poverty and inclusion in India?
questions.explanation: Current account deficit can lead to reduction in economic growth, increasing poverty.

questions.question: What is the primary objective of fiscal policy in the context of poverty reduction in India?
options: B. To increase economic growth and create jobs
C. To stabilize prices and control inflation
questions.explanation: Fiscal policy aims to promote economic growth, create jobs, and reduce poverty. Increased economic activity helps in generating employment opportunities.

questions.question: Which of the following monetary policy tools is used by the Reserve Bank of India to control inflation?
questions.explanation: The RBI uses various tools to control inflation, including Open Market Operations, Bank Rate, and Cash Reserve Ratio. These tools help in regulating money supply and credit.

questions.question: What is the impact of rupee depreciation on India's trade balance?
options: A. It improves the trade balance by increasing exports
B. It worsens the trade balance by increasing imports
C. It has no impact on the trade balance
questions.explanation: Rupee depreciation makes exports cheaper, increasing demand and exports. This improves the trade balance.

questions.question: Which of the following is a social indicator of poverty?
questions.explanation: Social indicators of poverty include per capita income, Human Development Index, and Gini Coefficient. These indicators help in assessing the poverty level and inequality.

questions.question: What is the objective of sterilized interventions by the Reserve Bank of India?
options: A. To reduce foreign exchange reserves
B. To stabilize the exchange rate
questions.explanation: Sterilized interventions aim to stabilize the exchange rate by neutralizing the impact of foreign capital inflows on money supply. This helps in maintaining economic stability.

questions.question: Which of the following is a component of the balance of payments?
questions.explanation: The balance of payments includes the Current Account, Capital Account, and Financial Account. These accounts record all international transactions.

questions.question: What is the impact of agricultural reforms on poverty reduction in India?
options: A. It increases poverty by reducing agricultural subsidies
B. It reduces poverty by increasing agricultural productivity
C. It has no impact on poverty
questions.explanation: Agricultural reforms aim to increase productivity, reduce poverty, and improve livelihoods. Increased productivity leads to higher incomes and reduced poverty.

questions.question: Which of the following is an inflation factor in India?
options: C. Rise in international commodity prices
questions.explanation: Inflation in India is influenced by various factors, including demand and supply imbalance, increase in money supply, and rise in international commodity prices. These factors contribute to price increases.

questions.question: What is the objective of trade balance in the context of poverty reduction?
options: A. To reduce imports and increase exports
B. To increase imports and reduce exports
C. To stabilize the exchange rate
questions.explanation: A favorable trade balance helps in increasing foreign exchange reserves, which can be used for poverty reduction initiatives. It also promotes economic growth.

questions.question: Which of the following fiscal indicators is used to assess the government's fiscal stance?
questions.explanation: Fiscal indicators such as Fiscal Deficit, Revenue Deficit, and Primary Deficit are used to assess the government's fiscal stance and its impact on the economy. These indicators help in evaluating fiscal policy.

questions.question: What is the primary objective of monetary policy in reducing poverty and promoting inclusion in India?
questions.explanation: Monetary policy aims to control inflation and promote economic growth, which are crucial for reducing poverty and promoting inclusion.

questions.question: Which of the following agricultural reforms is likely to have the most significant impact on reducing rural poverty in India?
questions.explanation: Minimum Support Price (MSP) can help increase farmers' incomes, thereby reducing rural poverty.

questions.question: What is the relationship between trade balance and poverty reduction in India?
options: A) Trade surplus leads to poverty reduction
B) Trade deficit leads to poverty reduction
C) Trade balance has no impact on poverty
D) Trade surplus can lead to job creation and poverty reduction
questions.explanation: A trade surplus can lead to job creation and increased economic activity, contributing to poverty reduction.

questions.question: Which of the following components of the balance of payments is most closely related to poverty reduction in India?
questions.explanation: The current account, which includes trade and remittances, is closely related to poverty reduction, as it affects employment and income opportunities.

questions.question: What is the relationship between inflation factors and poverty reduction in India?
options: A) High inflation leads to poverty reduction
B) Low inflation leads to poverty reduction
C) Inflation has no impact on poverty
D) High inflation can lead to increased poverty
questions.explanation: High inflation can lead to increased poverty, as it erodes the purchasing power of the poor.

questions.question: Which of the following fiscal policies is most effective in promoting inclusion and reducing poverty in India?
questions.explanation: Progressive taxation and subsidies can help reduce inequality and promote inclusion, thereby reducing poverty.

questions.question: Which of the following fiscal indicators is most closely related to poverty reduction?
questions.explanation: Fiscal deficit is related to poverty reduction as it indicates government borrowing.

questions.question: What is the impact of rupee depreciation on poverty in India?
questions.explanation: Rupee depreciation increases import costs and can lead to higher prices.

questions.question: Which agricultural reform can help reduce poverty among farmers in India?
questions.explanation: Reforms that increase farmers' access to markets and credit can help.

questions.question: Which of the following trade balances can indicate a reduction in poverty?
questions.explanation: A trade surplus can indicate increased exports and economic growth.

questions.question: What is the impact of sterilized interventions on poverty reduction in India?
questions.explanation: Sterilized interventions can help control inflation.

questions.question: What is the impact of balance of payments components on poverty reduction in India?
questions.explanation: A stable balance of payments can promote economic growth.

questions.question: Which of the following is a key factor in determining poverty levels in India?
questions.explanation: GDP growth rate is a key factor in determining poverty levels.

questions.question: Which of the following is a monetary policy tool used by the Reserve Bank of India to control inflation?
questions.explanation: Open market operations is a monetary policy tool used by RBI to control inflation.

questions.question: Which of the following is a social indicator of poverty in India?
questions.explanation: Human Development Index is a social indicator that measures poverty and well-being in India.

questions.question: What is the effect of sterilized interventions by the RBI on the money supply in the economy?
options: C) No impact on money supply
questions.explanation: Sterilized interventions by RBI decrease the money supply in the economy.

questions.question: Which of the following agricultural reforms aims to increase farmers' income and reduce poverty?
questions.explanation: All the above options are agricultural reforms that aim to increase farmers' income and reduce poverty.

questions.question: What is the impact of inflation on the poor in India?
options: C) No impact on purchasing power
questions.explanation: Inflation decreases the purchasing power of the poor in India.

questions.question: Which of the following is a component of the balance of payments in India?
questions.explanation: Trade balance is a component of the balance of payments in India.

questions.question: What is the effect of an increase in foreign investment on poverty in India?
questions.explanation: An increase in foreign investment can lead to a decrease in poverty in India.

questions.question: Which of the following fiscal indicators is used to measure the government's effort to reduce poverty in India?
questions.explanation: Social sector expenditure is a fiscal indicator that measures the government's effort to reduce poverty in India.

questions.question: Which of the following fiscal indicators is most closely related to poverty and inclusion in India?
options: A) Fiscal deficit as a percentage of GDP
B) Revenue deficit as a percentage of GDP
C) Primary deficit as a percentage of GDP
D) Social sector expenditure as a percentage of total expenditure
questions.explanation: Social sector expenditure has a direct impact on poverty and inclusion. It includes spending on health, education, and welfare programs.

questions.question: Which of the following agricultural reforms is likely to have the most significant impact on poverty and inclusion in rural India?
options: A) Introduction of new crop insurance schemes
B) Implementation of minimum support prices for farmers
C) Promotion of organic farming and sustainable agriculture
D) Liberalization of agricultural trade and reduction of subsidies
questions.explanation: Minimum support prices ensure a stable income for farmers, reducing poverty and income inequality. It provides a safety net for farmers and promotes agricultural growth.

questions.question: What is the expected impact of a increase in foreign direct investment (FDI) on poverty and inclusion in India?
options: A) Increase in economic growth and reduction in poverty
B) Decrease in economic growth and increase in poverty
C) No impact on economic growth and poverty
D) Increase in income inequality and exacerbation of poverty
questions.explanation: An increase in FDI can lead to an increase in economic growth, creating jobs and reducing poverty. It can also lead to an increase in foreign exchange reserves.

questions.question: What is the primary objective of monetary policy tools in the context of poverty-inclusion in the Indian economy?
options: A) To control inflation and stabilize prices
B) To promote economic growth and reduce unemployment
C) To increase foreign investment and improve trade balance
D) To reduce fiscal deficit and improve government revenue
questions.explanation: Monetary policy aims to control inflation, which disproportionately affects the poor. Price stability is crucial for poverty-inclusion.

questions.question: What is the expected impact of agricultural reforms on poverty-inclusion in rural India?
options: A) Increased rural poverty due to reduced government support
B) Reduced rural poverty due to increased agricultural productivity
C) No impact on rural poverty as agricultural reforms are irrelevant
D) Increased urban poverty due to migration of rural labor
questions.explanation: Agricultural reforms can increase productivity, leading to higher incomes and reduced poverty. This can have a positive impact on poverty-inclusion.

questions.question: Which factor is most likely to contribute to inflation in India, exacerbating poverty?
options: A) Increase in money supply due to fiscal stimulus
B) Decrease in agricultural production due to weather conditions
C) Increase in foreign investment leading to higher demand
D) Decrease in global oil prices reducing production costs
questions.explanation: An increase in money supply can lead to inflation, reducing the purchasing power of the poor. This exacerbates poverty.

questions.question: What is the relationship between trade balances and poverty-inclusion in India?
options: A) A trade surplus leads to increased poverty due to reduced imports
B) A trade deficit leads to reduced poverty due to increased exports
C) A trade surplus leads to reduced poverty due to increased foreign exchange reserves
D) A trade deficit has no impact on poverty-inclusion
questions.explanation: A trade surplus can lead to increased foreign exchange reserves, which can be used to fund poverty-reduction programs. This can have a positive impact on poverty-inclusion.

questions.question: Which social indicator is most relevant to assessing poverty-inclusion in India?
questions.explanation: The poverty headcount ratio directly measures the percentage of the population living below the poverty line. It is a key indicator of poverty-inclusion.

questions.question: What is the impact of rupee depreciation on poverty-inclusion in India?
options: A) Increased poverty due to higher import prices and inflation
B) Reduced poverty due to increased export competitiveness
C) No impact on poverty as rupee depreciation is irrelevant
D) Increased poverty due to reduced foreign investment
questions.explanation: Rupee depreciation can lead to higher import prices and inflation, reducing the purchasing power of the poor. This exacerbates poverty.

questions.question: Which of the following is a consequence of sterilized interventions by the RBI?
options: A) Reduced money supply and higher interest rates
B) Increased money supply and lower interest rates
C) No impact on money supply or interest rates
D) Increased foreign exchange reserves and reduced inflation
questions.explanation: Sterilized interventions involve selling government securities to reduce money supply and mitigate inflationary pressures. This can lead to higher interest rates.

questions.question: What is the relationship between balance of payments components and poverty-inclusion in India?
options: A) A current account deficit leads to increased poverty due to reduced foreign exchange reserves
B) A capital account surplus leads to reduced poverty due to increased foreign investment
C) A current account surplus leads to reduced poverty due to increased foreign exchange reserves
D) A capital account deficit has no impact on poverty-inclusion
questions.explanation: A current account surplus can lead to increased foreign exchange reserves, which can be used to fund poverty-reduction programs. This can have a positive impact on poverty-inclusion.

questions.question: Which of the following is a challenge in implementing poverty-reduction programs in India?
options: A) Inadequate funding and lack of political will
B) Inefficient targeting and leakage of benefits
C) Insufficient data and lack of monitoring
questions.explanation: All the options are challenges in implementing poverty-reduction programs in India. Inadequate funding, inefficient targeting, and insufficient data can all hinder the effectiveness of these programs.

questions.question: What is the primary objective of monetary policy in the context of poverty inclusion in India?
questions.explanation: Monetary policy aims to control inflation and promote economic growth, which can help reduce poverty.

questions.question: Which of the following fiscal indicators is most closely related to poverty inclusion?
questions.explanation: Fiscal deficit indicates government borrowing, which can be used for social welfare and poverty alleviation programs.

questions.question: Which factor is most likely to contribute to inflation in India, affecting poverty inclusion?
questions.explanation: Increase in minimum support price can lead to higher food prices and inflation.

questions.question: What is the relationship between trade balance and poverty inclusion in India?
options: Trade balance has no impact on poverty
questions.explanation: A trade surplus can indicate higher exports and economic growth, which can help reduce poverty.

questions.question: Which social indicator is most closely related to poverty inclusion in India?
questions.explanation: Poverty Headcount Ratio directly measures the percentage of population living below the poverty line.

questions.question: What is the purpose of sterilized interventions by the RBI in the context of poverty inclusion?
questions.explanation: Sterilized interventions aim to stabilize the exchange rate and control inflation.

questions.question: Which component of the balance of payments is most closely related to poverty inclusion in India?
questions.explanation: Current Account includes trade balance and remittances, which can impact poverty.

questions.question: What is the expected impact of increase in foreign investment on poverty inclusion in India?
questions.explanation: Increase in foreign investment can lead to economic growth and job creation, reducing poverty.
